transcript:
DR: Daniel, thanks for coming in. Your labs from last week came back, so let's go through them. How have you been feeling overall?
PT: Pretty okay. Tired sometimes. The main thing is my A1c, right?
DR: Right. Your A1c came back at 8.1. Last time it was 7.4, so it's crept up a bit.
PT: That's the sugar average thing. Higher than we want.
DR: Exactly, the three-month average. Our goal for you is under 7. So I want to adjust your metformin — you're on 500 milligrams twice a day now, let's move you to 1000 milligrams twice a day.
PT: Double it. Morning and night, with food?
DR: Yes, with meals — that helps with the stomach upset it can cause. If you get some loose stool the first week, that usually settles.
PT: Okay. One thing I wanted to mention — the last month or so my feet have been kind of numb and tingly, especially at night. Like pins and needles.
DR: Okay. Let me ask — are you checking your sugars at home regularly?
PT: Most mornings. They're usually around 150, 160.
DR: Those morning numbers line up with the A1c. Let's also make sure we get your eyes checked — when was your last retinal exam?
PT: Uh, over a year ago I think.
DR: Let's get that scheduled, diabetes can affect the eyes so we want a yearly look. I'll put in the referral.
PT: Alright.
DR: And keep working on the diet side — cutting back on the sugary drinks especially would help those morning numbers a lot.
PT: Yeah, I've been having a soda with lunch. I can switch to water.
DR: That alone can make a real difference. Let's recheck your A1c in three months and see where the higher metformin dose gets us.
PT: Three months, okay.
DR: I'll have the nurse set up the eye referral and the lab. Anything else on your mind today?
PT: No, I think that's it.
DR: Great. Keep up the home checks and we'll talk in three months.
PT: Thanks, doctor.
note:
**Subjective:** Mr. Okafor is a 57-year-old man with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, and overweight, here for routine diabetes follow-up. Reports feeling generally well, occasional fatigue. Checks fasting glucose most mornings, typically 150–160. Reports one sugary beverage daily.

**Objective:** A1c 8.1% (prior 7.4%). BP at goal on lisinopril.

**Assessment & Plan:**
- Type 2 diabetes, suboptimal control (A1c 8.1%, up from 7.4%). Increase metformin from 500 mg BID to 1000 mg BID, taken with meals; counseled on transient GI upset.
- Dietary counseling: reduce sugar-sweetened beverages, substitute water.
- Overdue diabetic retinal exam — referral placed for annual screening.
- Recheck A1c in 3 months.
- Continue lisinopril; BP at goal.
after_visit_summary:
Visit summary

What we discussed
• Diabetes — A1c has gone up
• Adjusting your diabetes medication

Next steps
• Increase metformin to 1000 mg twice a day, with meals.
• Cut back on sugary drinks; switch to water.
• Get your yearly diabetic eye exam (referral placed).
• Recheck A1c in 3 months.
• Keep continue checking your sugars in the morning.
